questions.question_text: What is the WB-1 form used for in Wisconsin?
questions.correct_answer: Residential Listing Contract
distractors: Residential Offer to Purchase
Buyer Agency Agreement
Counter-Offer to Purchase
questions.explanation: The WB-1 is the REEB-approved Residential Listing Contract — Exclusive Right to Sell. It establishes the listing agreement between the seller and the listing firm.

questions.question_text: A seller asks their agent what the standard commission rate is. How should the agent respond?
questions.correct_answer: Explain that there is no standard commission rate
distractors: Quote the rate that most agents in the area charge
Refer the seller to the REEB for guidance
State that commissions are set by the MLS
questions.explanation: Since 2024, all WB listing forms include the statement: 'There is no standard market commission rate.' Commission rates are always negotiable between the seller and the listing firm.

questions.question_text: Under the updated WB-1, what must the listing contract address regarding compensation to the buyer's firm?
questions.correct_answer: Whether the seller authorizes disclosure of such compensation
distractors: The exact commission split with the buyer's firm
That the seller must pay the buyer's agent directly
That the MLS will determine the buyer's firm compensation
questions.explanation: The updated WB-1 includes a statement asking whether the seller authorizes the listing firm to disclose if the seller is offering compensation to a firm other than the listing firm.

questions.question_text: Which listing form would be used for a farmland sale in Wisconsin?
questions.correct_answer: WB-2
distractors: WB-1
WB-3
WB-5
questions.explanation: WB-2 is the Farm Listing Contract. WB-1 is for residential, WB-3 is for vacant land, WB-4 is for condominiums, WB-5 is for commercial, and WB-6 is for business opportunities.

questions.question_text: What key information must be specified in a WB-1 listing contract?
questions.correct_answer: Listing price, commission terms, and contract duration
distractors: Only the listing price and agent name
The buyer's expected financing type
The property's assessed tax value
questions.explanation: The WB-1 must specify the listing price, commission terms (including the 'no standard rate' disclosure), and the duration of the listing agreement, along with other essential terms.

questions.question_text: When must a Wisconsin buyer agency agreement (WB-36) be signed?
questions.correct_answer: Before the licensee provides brokerage services
distractors: Within 48 hours of the first showing
Before the buyer writes an offer
At the time of closing
questions.explanation: Wisconsin has long required written buyer agency agreements before providing brokerage services. The WB-36 must be signed before the licensee begins working on the buyer's behalf.

questions.question_text: The compensation in a WB-36 buyer agency agreement must be:
questions.correct_answer: Objectively ascertainable
distractors: Set at the MLS standard rate
Approved by REEB in advance
Identical to the listing commission
questions.explanation: Compensation in a WB-36 must be objectively ascertainable — meaning a specific percentage, flat dollar amount, or hourly rate. Vague terms like 'customary rate' are insufficient.

questions.question_text: What is notable about Wisconsin's buyer agency agreement requirement compared to other states?
questions.correct_answer: Wisconsin required it decades before the 2024 NAR settlement
distractors: Wisconsin was the last state to adopt it
Wisconsin only requires it for commercial transactions
Wisconsin allows verbal buyer agency agreements
questions.explanation: Wisconsin has required written buyer agency agreements (WB-36) for decades, long before the 2024 NAR settlement that prompted many other states to adopt similar requirements.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the WB-50 form?
questions.correct_answer: Pre-Agency Showing Agreement
distractors: Vacant Land Offer to Purchase
Cancellation and Mutual Release
Multiple Counter-Proposal
questions.explanation: The WB-50 is a Pre-Agency Showing Agreement that allows a buyer to view properties before signing a full buyer agency agreement (WB-36). It creates a limited scope relationship.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants to view a property but is unsure about signing a full buyer agency agreement. Which form addresses this?
questions.correct_answer: WB-50 Pre-Agency Showing Agreement
distractors: WB-36 Buyer Agency Agreement
WB-11 Offer to Purchase
WB-41 Notice Relating to Offer
questions.explanation: The WB-50 Pre-Agency Showing Agreement is designed for this situation. It allows limited property viewing without establishing a full agency relationship, giving the buyer time to decide.

questions.question_text: Since August 2024, how are buyer agent commissions handled on the MLS in Wisconsin?
questions.correct_answer: MLS no longer includes offers of compensation to buyer's agents
distractors: MLS requires a minimum buyer agent commission
MLS still displays the seller's offered commission
Buyer agents cannot receive compensation at all
questions.explanation: Following the NAR settlement (effective August 17, 2024), MLS listings no longer include offers of compensation to buyer's agents. Listing firms must obtain written seller approval for any compensation to the buyer's firm.

questions.question_text: A listing agent wants to offer compensation to the buyer's firm from closing proceeds. What is required?
questions.correct_answer: Written seller approval for the compensation
distractors: Only verbal seller agreement at closing
REEB must pre-approve the arrangement
No approval needed if disclosed on MLS
questions.explanation: Post-NAR settlement, listing firms must obtain written seller approval before paying compensation to the buyer's firm. This ensures sellers know about and authorize any commission sharing.

questions.question_text: What statement about commission rates must now appear in all WB forms?
questions.correct_answer: There is no standard market commission rate
distractors: Commission rates are set by REEB annually
The standard rate is determined by local MLS
Commissions are regulated by state statute
questions.explanation: Since 2024, all WB forms include the statement: 'There is no standard market commission rate.' This reinforces that commissions are always negotiable and not fixed by any entity.

questions.question_text: Which 2024 addition now appears in all WB offers to purchase?
questions.correct_answer: A wire fraud warning
distractors: A radon testing requirement
An appraisal contingency clause
A mandatory home warranty provision
questions.explanation: The 2024 WB form updates added wire fraud warnings to all offers to purchase, alerting parties to verify wire transfer instructions through trusted channels to prevent fraud.

questions.question_text: Under 2025 Act 69, what is prohibited starting January 1, 2027?
questions.correct_answer: Firm-to-firm compensation on 1-4 unit residential sales
distractors: All buyer agency agreements within the state
Commission rates above 3% on residential sales
Listing agents advertising properties on the internet
questions.explanation: 2025 Act 69 prohibits firms from accepting compensation from another firm for brokerage services on 1-4 unit residential transactions. Referral fees and finder's fees are still allowed.

questions.question_text: Under 2025 Act 69, when must a listed property be advertised on the public internet?
questions.correct_answer: Within 1 business day unless seller opts out
distractors: Within 5 business days of listing activation
Within 24 hours with absolutely no exceptions
Only if the seller specifically requests marketing
questions.explanation: 2025 Act 69 creates a public marketing default: listings must be advertised on the public internet within 1 business day. The seller may opt out with a written acknowledgment that the property won't appear on public platforms.

questions.question_text: Under 2025 Act 69, what happens to an agreement that violates the firm-to-firm compensation prohibition?
questions.correct_answer: The agreement is void and unenforceable
distractors: A $5,000 penalty is assessed per violation
The firm receives a written warning only
The agreement is valid until challenged in court
questions.explanation: 2025 Act 69 states that any agreement violating the firm-to-firm compensation prohibition on 1-4 unit residential properties is void and unenforceable. This is a strong deterrent against non-compliance.

questions.question_text: Under 2025 Act 69, what type of compensation between firms is still permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Referral fees and finder's fees
distractors: Commissions up to 1% of sale price
Flat fees under $500 per transaction
Performance bonuses based on sales volume
questions.explanation: While 2025 Act 69 prohibits firm-to-firm compensation for brokerage services on 1-4 unit residential properties, it expressly permits referral fees and finder's fees between firms.

questions.question_text: 2025 Act 69 requires technology disclosure when:
questions.correct_answer: Technology materially alters the property's presentation
distractors: A virtual tour is posted on any real estate website
Digital signatures are used on any official WB form
Electronic lockboxes are installed on a listed property
questions.explanation: 2025 Act 69 requires disclosure when technology materially alters the property's presentation in marketing materials. This addresses concerns about AI-enhanced photos or virtual staging that could mislead buyers.

questions.question_text: When did the updated WB-36 buyer agency agreement become mandatory?
questions.correct_answer: August 15, 2024
distractors: January 1, 2024
July 1, 2024
October 1, 2024
questions.explanation: The updated WB-36 had an optional use date of July 1, 2024, and became mandatory on August 15, 2024. The updates included compensation transparency language aligning with the NAR settlement.
